entry.title: 2H 2024 Release Highlights for the Compensation Module
entry.content:
The 2nd Half 2024 Release of SAP SuccessFactors is rolled out on Preview environments since October 14 (Production: November 15-17) and is bringing tons of great new features for the Compensation module, please find my top 5 below with demo recordings as well as the list of all new features here !

 

 

✅Executive Review Access Based on Route Step

Huge time-saver for Compensation administrators that now have full independence from security admins (RBP) when running the compensation cycle.

Please find a 23' demo with insights here: https://youtu.be/f7OhtfacS-k 

 

 

✅AI-assisted writing for Compensation

AI is here for the Compensation module to help write quality texts quickly to justify increases or spot awards ! very exciting.

Please find a 3' demo here: https://youtu.be/6yM0d7g_sxw 

AI-assisted Writing is available through our Embedded AI offering across many areas of the SuccessFactors suite. Please note: AI-assisted Writing is not available through Joule.

Additional details:
✅ AI-assisted Writing is a Premium AI Feature within SuccessFactors Business AI.
✅ Thus, AI-assisted Writing requires SAP AI Units per use.
✅ That includes the actions available such as Enhance Writing, Change Tone, and more.

More information can be found here.

 

✅Editable Statements Transport

Manually re-creating compensation statements in a target environment as part of configuration migration was one of the few remaining time-consuming items for implementation partners. It is now solved with this new enhancement that allows us to export standard statements as XML and import them into a target environment while keeping them as editable in the Statement Designer UI. With the support for multiple languages as well as the rich text editor added in 1H 2024 standard statements are now the best they've ever been. 

Please find a 5' demo with insights here: https://youtu.be/7eGOgC5fo1Q 

 

If you are going to 2024 SuccessConnect in Lisbon, don't miss my colleague Nestor's session where he will show an example of a great-looking statement he was able to build leveraging this 1H 2024 enhancement:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 


✅API for Compensation Data (read)

Allows us to feed planning data to other systems in real time (I got the requirement in my most recent implementation). 

Enhancement official page.

 



✅Publish to MDF for Total Comp templates

What I considered the latest major gap of Total Comp has been addressed and we can now build end-to-end complex workarounds for specific requirements.

Enhancement official page. 

For a demo of publish to MDF (first brought to Compensation templates in 2H 2021) please see this blog.

 

 

 

 

All the best,

Xavier

 

(If you found this blog and its recordings useful please consider giving it a Kudo)

entry.title: CDS combine lines into one
entry.content:
Hi all, 
I need some help. 
I have a CDS view with several lines and I want to combine this lines into one. 
Example

MaterialColumn1Column2Column3
111111Value  
111111 Value 
111111  Value
222222 Value2 
222222  Value2

The expected result:

MaterialColumn1Column2Column3

222222 Value2Value2

All the Columns are string fields. 

Maybe someone can give me a tip on how to implement this in CDS

Thanks and best regards, 
Helge

entry.title: Accelerating the journey to S/4 HANA
entry.content:
You would be hard-pressed to find a SAP customer who does not have the migration to S/4 HANA in the forefront of his mind. Companies are undertaking this journey at their own pace and many organisations are at different stages of that transformation. With the 2027 deadline looming, it’s high time to step up the pace to avoid getting cut off of maintenance of the platform. A solid quality assurance strategy is key in making it to the finish line in time.

The pros and cons of an S/4 HANA migration

Reading the magazines and websites of SAP user groups around the world, it is clear that many SAP users are not undertaking the migration wholeheartedly. They realise the effort will be time-consuming and is loaded with complexity. Over the years, organisations have added custom code to their SAP instances and now face the daunting task of reviewing or rewriting these additions when they move from an on-premise implementation to the cloud.

However, the migration does bring multiple benefits. For starters, it provides companies with the opportunity to come clean with all these customisations. In reality, many of these bespoke additions are no longer in use and might as well be left behind.

S/4 HANA also brings additional benefits: the in-memory database offers real-time analytics, improved performance and enhanced capabilities. A modern and intuitive user interface boosts productivity by optimising the user experience. S/4 HANA comes with advanced technologies like artificial intelligence, machine learning, Internet of Things, offering businesses the potential to innovate faster. And by using the migration as an opportunity to review and simplify business processes, organisations will be able to operate with greater agility.

How can I speed up the migration from ECC to S/4 HANA?

Patience is a virtue, but there are more reasons to accelerate the journey to S/4 HANA than reasons to stall. Here are some ways in which you can speed up the cycle:

1. Build an integrated plan

If it is true that Abraham Lincoln once said “"Give me six hours to chop down a tree and I will spend the first four sharpening the axe," he would make the perfect project manager for an S/4 HANA migration. Preparation is everything when it comes to such a huge undertaking, and sometimes you may want to invest some time first and start slower – enabling you to move faster afterwards. So start by thinking about the aim of the migration, how will it support the strategic goals of the company? What are the most important business processes? Ensure you have all these answers when you build a plan. And only move forward once the integrated plan has been approved by all the stakeholders.

2. Involve the business, early on

A SAP implementation is there to support the business and all the business-critical processes inside an organisation. Sooner or later, the migration will appear on the agenda of the business leaders in that organisation. By involving them from the start, you can build critical alignment between business and IT, and avoid rework because senior management does not agree with the path that you are taking. By involving business from the start, you will get their buy-in and you will save valuable time.

3. Shift left and move faster

Traditionally, much of the quality assurance process comes late in the project, and the people who get to use the system will only be confronted with it at the very last moment, when User Acceptance Testing is scheduled. For a couple of years already, it has become best practice to start testing early on, a practice that has been dubbed ‘shift-left’ testing. The advantages of testing early are manifold: it will help you detect defects earlier, eliminating rework, and driving down costs. At the same time, by moving UAT forward, you will gain more confidence with the users. User confidence is a great asset to change management and helps reduce resistance to change.

4. Automate your testing

Migrating all applications, all data, all interfaces, all integrations… is a mammoth undertaking and there is simply no way that you can perform all quality assurance tasks by manual testing alone. Both TTC and SAP recommend automating the testing process , either by using Tricentis Test Automation (TTA) which is part of SAP Enterprise Support, or by investing in SAP Enterprise Continuous Testing (ECT) by Tricentis, which offers more functionality than TTA and also supports non-SAP technologies. These tools come recommended by SAP (ECT is part of SAP’s Solution Extension portfolio) but, of course, customers are free to choose any other solution, including open source tools. Test automation reduces testing cost and time and guarantees faster feedback cycles, while also offering more scalability, maximising test coverage and allowing more test case reuse. In my experience, the more mature a quality assurance department is, the more they use test automation.

5. Start with the end in mind

When you build your integrated plan, you know that assuring the quality of S/4 HANA does not stop after the migration. There are sure to be changes because the business requires them, or because new projects are started. This means you will also need to set up regression tests to ensure these changes have no impact on other parts of the application. Besides these business-driven changes, organisations will also receive regular maintenance updates and upgrades that also require immediate testing. By taking this into account early in the process, this will save you valuable time in the long run.

6. Don’t test too much

This may seem like a counterintuitive thing for a testing consultancy to say, but there is no need to test everything all the time. Of course, you will continually test your key business workflows, for instance your month-end, quarter-end or year-end closing. These need to be part of your regression test pack. But there are less important processes that do not require constant testing. Of the thousand tests in your regression pack, there may be 20 tests that are crucial and 980 that do not require daily attention. Using a tool like LiveCompare  (called Change Impact Analysis in the SAP catalog) helps u decide what areas in S/4 HANA may be most at risk and merit testing efforts the most.

7. Get an experienced, independent, impartial view

There are many moving parts in an S/4 HANA integration. Not from a technical perspective, but also from a human point of view. A failed migration will have huge business consequences, so you need to get things right from the first time. And this is where a consultancy like TTC Global comes in. We have plenty of experience with SAP, with the Tricentis tools that SAP favors and we have a laser-sharp focus on just one thing: quality assurance. As an external expert, we can bring all stakeholders together and ensure a good business/IT alignment as we speak the language of both these groups inside your organisation. We will not only work with all the internal stakeholders, but also with other third parties that you are working with. By getting us on board from the start, we can help build that integrated plan based on the best practices that have been tested and proven in previous projects.

entry.title: Modeling SAP BTP in LeanIX - An Example using BTP Custom App for S2P Optimization
entry.content:
Let us start with an use case initiative of using SAP BTP for Optimizing Supply Chain Operations under a broader Source to Pay heading with the goal of delivering a end to end Supplier Freight Tracking, Monitoring & Compliance app on BTP . Our primary objective is to adhere to fundamental clean core principles throughout our journey and utilize SAP LeanIX to model this within the context of SAP Enterprise Architecture. Given our focus on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) modeling using LeanIX as the centerpiece of this article, we will not delve into the intricacies of S/4HANA modeling with LeanIX.

Objective: To provide a more comprehensive enterprise perspective, we could phrase our Objective as ‘Optimize Supply Chain Operations.’ This broader phrasing encompasses other related initiatives that contribute to this goal. Furthermore, this Objective can be divided into specific, tangible benefits that we anticipate achieving. For instance,

  • Increase freight visibility: Achieve 90% real-time tracking of all inbound supplier shipments.
  • Reduce freight costs: Decrease transportation costs by 5% through optimized routing and carrier selection.
  • Improve on-time delivery: Increase on-time delivery performance from suppliers by 10%.
  • Enhance collaboration: Enable seamless information sharing between suppliers, carriers, and internal stakeholders.

Initiative: According to the LeanIX v4 Meta Model, Initiatives are designed to support Objectives. In this specific scenario, we have two Initiatives that contribute to our overarching Objective of 'Optimizing Supply Chain Operations.

Initiative 1: Supplier BTP Apps Development: This initiative focuses on the design, development, and testing of the BTP application for Supplier Freights Tracking, Monitoring & Governance. This initiative ensures suppliers are trained and integrated onto the new platform.

Initiative 2: System Integration: This initiative connects the new application with existing ERP and TMS systems.

Initiatives can be further divided into Subtypes such as Programs and Projects. However, for the sake of simplicity, we will focus solely on the Initiative level in this article.

 

After establishing our Objectives and desired Outcomes, we can proceed to model the Platform, Initiative, Organization, and Business Capability. These elements are directly connected to our stated Objective

Business Capability: Business capabilities structure and describe the essential elements (capabilities) that an organization needs to fulfill its business model.

So we consider following Business Capabilities that are benefitting from our Objective and Initiatives. This is just a sample set of Business Capabilities, which are linked to the Objective.

 

  • Supplier Qualification: This capability covers all processes involved in identifying, evaluating, and onboarding new suppliers. This includes gathering information, assessing risks, verifying qualifications, and negotiating contracts.
  • Supplier Performance Management: This capability involves monitoring and evaluating supplier performance based on key metrics like quality, delivery, and compliance.
  • Freight Procurement: This capability covers the sourcing and contracting of freight services from carriers, including rate negotiation, carrier selection, and contract management.
  • Shipment Planning and Execution: This capability involves planning and executing the physical movement of goods, including route optimization, load planning, and shipment scheduling.
  • Freight Planning and Optimization: This capability focuses on real-time monitoring of shipments, providing insights into location, status, and estimated arrival times.

Business Context: Business contexts describe on a more granular level what an organization does to achieve its business goals; they can be clustered and analyzed by, e.g., products, processes, customer journeys, and value streams. In this article, we list out few Value Streams & Business Processes which are directly affected/related to the Initiatives and Organizations.

 

  • Purchase Requirements Processing:Initiate the procurement process by creating purchase requisitions for goods or services. Route requisitions for approval based on predefined workflows. Generate purchase orders from approved requisitions.
  • Purchase Order Processing:Generate purchase orders based on approved requisitions, specifying details such as materials, quantities, prices, and delivery dates. Send purchase orders to suppliers through various channels, including EDI, email, or supplier portals. Monitor the status of purchase orders and track confirmations from suppliers.
  • Freight Order Collaboration:Determine optimal shipping routes, modes of transportation, and carriers. Monitor the location and status of shipments using tracking systems and technologies.
  • Goods Receipt :Physically receive goods at the designated delivery location. Confirm that received goods match the purchase order in terms of quantity, quality, and specifications. Update the system with details of the goods receipt, including quantities, storage locations, and batch numbers.
  • Invoice Processing: Receiving and verifying supplier invoices, ensuring accuracy and compliance with purchase orders and contracts.
  • Payment Processing : Initiating and executing payments to suppliers according to agreed-upon payment terms and methods.

 

Organization: In LeanIX Meta Model, Organizations represent the users/owners/Departments that own Objectives, Platforms, Initiatives, Business Contexts and IT Components. Organization uses Applications for day to day Operations and activities.

We have identified the following Organizations to be modeled in LeanIX as they are the primary beneficiaries of the BTP initiative. Please note that this is a representative sample for the purpose of this article and does not constitute an exhaustive list.

 

  • Global Procurement OU Local
  • Procurement OU
  • BTP Center of Excellence
  • Global Finance OU
  • Local Finance OU

 

It is beneficial to categorize Organizational units as Global (Headquarters) and Local (Country or Region-specific) because when modeling Business Contexts (for example, Business Processes), we can assign both Global and Local Business Processes to the corresponding Organizations that own them. This approach aligns with the traditional SAP concept of Global Templates and Local Templates.

Platform: Platforms provide functionality consumed by other applications and microservices to accelerate delivery or provide consistent/common functionality and support a set of capabilities. In the context of BTP modelling following 2 Platforms ( Business Technology Platform & ERP Platform) are considered in the Platform factsheet.

 

Following Best Practices laid out in the LeanIX Documentation has to be considered.

 

  • Define objectives and intiatives to build up your SAP BTP instance to your strategic ERP platform of the future. You can also link other objectives and initiatives to your platform fact sheets.
  • Link fact sheets from the business architecture layer (organization, business capability, business context) to get a good understanding of the business value of your SAP BTP solutions.
  • Define your SAP BTP account structure with the platform fact sheet, which reflects SAP BTP’s logical governance structure. It consolidates all your SAP BTP Global and Subaccounts, showing responsibility and potentially costs for major BTP elements.
  • Model custom-built and deployed apps on SAP BTP with application fact sheets.
  • Connect these applications to the relevant PaaS services using IT component fact sheets and link them to underlying SAP ERP systems via interface fact sheets if they retrieve data from those systems.

 

Create a custom Tag group for tagging IaaS, PaaS, SaaS , the IT Components that are created and linked to Business Technology Platform are classified  using this custom Tags.

 

Generate a Landscape Report for the SAP Business Technology Platform.

 

Application: Applications are software systems or programs that process or analyze business data to support business tasks, processes, or aspects of an organization’s business model. The Supplier BTP Apps that we planned are  Applications in the context of LeanIX meta model. Also, the surrounding application ecosystems that BTP App interacts with are also modeled ( for example, S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Cloud Connector, SAP Integration Suite etc.

Some example Applications could be;

 

  • Supplier Freight Tracking Fiori App
  • Supplier Onboarding Fiori App
  • Supplier Performance & Governace Dashboard

 

Data Object: Data Objects are used in SAP LeanIX to analyze which data is processed by specific Applications and exchanged via specific Interfaces. In the SAP world, the common Data Objects are Master Data and Transactional Data. In this modelling example, we consider following  Master Data and Transactional Data.

 

  • Business Partner, Material Master, Storage Location, Shipping Points, Purchasing Info Records
  • Purchase Order, Purchase Requisition, Shipping Document, Delivery, Goods Receipts, Invoice Receipts

 

Hint : Create subtypes for factsheet Data Object, recommended to have at least Master Data and Transactional Data. This would be very useful while modeling the Data Flow diagrams.

 

Interfaces: Interfaces are connections between Applications. They model how data exchange happens between Applications. We consider HTTPS and RFC interfaces between BTP and S/4HANA Cloud PCE system.

Tip: If the organization is SAP heavy shop, then I suggest to have various subtypes for Interface factsheet like below;

 

IT Component: IT Components are building blocks of Applications that run, maintain, and change Applications.

 

  • IaaS: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) refers to virtualized computing resources provided over the internet. For example, SAP Data Center in our example where BTP PaaS is hosted.
  • PaaS: PaaS (Platform as a Service) refers to cloud platforms for developers to build and deploy applications. For Example, SAP BTP
  • SaaS: SaaS (Software as a Service) refers to software applications accessed over the internet on a subscription basis. For example, SAP Integration Suite.

 

Hint: The Reference catalog already has all the major BTP Services covered, while creating IT Component, you could link your factsheet with the item from Reference Catalog.

 

Here is one such useful Diagram view as output of modeling BTP.

This Diagram shows the linkage between the Objective to Initiative to the Application layer for a BTP initiative. It is suggested to maintain all relevant factsheet types while modeling. The factsheets will then unlock the ability to create numerous diagrams like this, showcasing the various layers and their relationships.

 

Hope this helps.

Regards,

Vivek Hegde

https://www.linkedin.com/in/vhegde1/

entry.title: How Do You Decide Your SAP S/4HANA Migration Path?
entry.content:
How do you decide your SAP S/4HANA migration path? This is an overwhelming challenge to any CTO organization who is on SAP ECC system.

 

There are a lot of factors hitting enterprises today, such as the adoption of a hybrid cloud architecture, native S/4HANA public cloud solutions and integrating SAP S/4HANA with other tools. The road to migrate an organization’s ERP architecture to SAP S/4HANA starts with understanding the hurdles one need to overcome in this transformation. The choice of the system integrator is equally as important, a partner that will turn your initiative into an executable project.

 

SAP S/4HANA is SAP’s intelligent, integrated ERP system that’s designed to run on SAP’s in-memory HANA database. It can be deployed on premises, in a public or private cloud, or in a hybrid environment. This replaces SAP ECC ERP suite of enterprise applications. ECC (Enterprise Core Components) was designed to run on a third-party database, such as Oracle or SQL Server. S/4HANA comes with SAP's own in-memory HANA database. Application Lifecycle Management periodically requires to upgrade or migrate to the latest of SAP ERP package. The move to S/4HANA provides once in a generation opportunities for customers to operate more efficiently and supports their larger digital journeys.

 

Since SAP is sunsetting SAP ECC 6.0 in 2027 (extended support till 2030), customers have to migrate SAP S/4HANA. Not meeting SAP’s deadlines will limit your ability to execute digital transformation, adapt new innovations including AI, able to use other 3rd party add-on software, leverage better user experience, improved performance, and reduced total cost of ownership. Additionally, since SAP S/4HANA is a more secure and easier to maintain platform. For all customers, small, medium, and large, it’s not a matter of “if” they have to migrate, but “when.”

 

However, it’s not always easy for organizations to move away from a business model that has been highly successful. It can also be challenging to commit to a new mindset, implement new technologies, and obtain the employee buy-in necessary to succeed. Organization change management will play an important role. It is no more an IT driven initiative, it will bring in a new operating model and a culture that brings together the business, the IT drivers, and talent. This intersection of technology, strategy, and talent is precisely where system integrators come in.

 

Migration to SAP S/4HANA comes with few option. If a company decide to implement S/4HANA public cloud SaaS solution, it will be completely greenfield. If a company decides to retain a in-premise or private cloud solution, there are three main approaches to roll out SAP S/4HANA: the greenfield approach, the brownfield approach, or a hybrid of the two.  Depending on a few key factors, such as your company’s use of SAP, the size and state of your data, the technical debt you carry, and your future business needs, the technical approach can be selected to build the business case. Customers use this opportunity to make the most of innovation, overhaul business processes, adopt standard out of box solutions and adopt cloud solutions. If customer have a heavy customization invested in SAP ECC and want to keep your existing data, and go live with a big bang approach, a system conversion is the way. This is also an opportunity to consolidate multiple SAP ECC instances. If customer decide to adapt a combination of the approaches in a phased program, that too is possible. There are enough opportunity for selecting the right approach tailored to suite you.

 

To help SAP customers decide between a Brownfield (system conversion) or Greenfield (new implementation) approach to transitioning to SAP S/4HANA, asking the right questions can help identify the most suitable path or evolve something in-between. These questions below will provide a comprehensive view of the customer’s needs and can guide the decision between Brownfield or Greenfield implementation.

 

 

AreaLeading Queries

1. Current System Evaluation

  1. How customized is your current SAP ERP system? (heavily customized systems may require more effort in a Brownfield approach) Do  you have a need to keep you existing solution enhancements or transaction data history?
  2. How well is your current system meeting business needs? Are there critical gaps or inefficiencies?
  3. Do you have a clear understanding of existing data quality? How much data cleansing would be required?

2. Business Strategy and Objectives

  1. What are your company’s long-term digital transformation goals?
  2. Are there significant business process changes required to align with S/4HANA's capabilities, or do you want to retain most existing processes?
  3. Do you plan to standardize your processes according to SAP best practices, or will you need to maintain a high level of customization?

3. Budget and Timeline

  1. What is your budget for the S/4HANA project? (Greenfield implementations can often be more expensive upfront, while Brownfield may have lower costs but less flexibility.)
  2. What are your timeline constraints? (Greenfield projects might take longer due to redesigning processes, while Brownfield conversions could be faster but require extensive testing.)
  3. Are you looking for a phased migration or a big-bang approach?

4. Risk Tolerance and Change Management

  1. How much disruption to business operations can your organization tolerate during the migration? (Business downtime)
  2. How comfortable is your organization with managing change? Are you prepared to undergo significant organizational and process changes (typically required in Greenfield)?
  3. How much invested your business will be in this transformation? What is your organization’s risk appetite? (Greenfield offers fresh opportunities but comes with the risk of business process re-engineering.)

5. Data Management

  1. How important is it to retain historical data in the new system? Are you planning on a complete or partial data migration?
  2. How mature is your data governance strategy? A Greenfield approach often requires more rigorous data management.
  3. How organized and clean are your master data, analytics data and transactional data? Do you archive your data?

6. Technical Architecture

  1. What is the state of your current system landscape? Is it up to date, or are there legacy systems that may not easily transition in a Brownfield scenario? Do you have a choice where your systems are hosted? Is your organization open for Hyperscale hosted solution?
  2. How scalable and flexible does your future SAP environment need to be? Are you interested in taking advantage of new technologies like SAP Fiori, AI, and IoT?
  3. Are there existing integrations with third-party systems that need to be considered, and how would they impact each approach?

7. Future-Proofing

  1. Do you want to future-proof your business processes for the next 10-15 years by adopting a completely new, SAP-standard model (Greenfield)?
  2. Is your organization sees value and is ready to adapt SAP best practices and take help of 'fit-to-standard' approach for process adaptation? 
  3. How do your business perceive your current system – as an Innovation Blocker or Key Asset?

8. Compliance and Regulatory Requirements

  1. Are there any regulatory, compliance, or industry-specific requirements that would impact the migration approach?
  2. Are you under any external pressure to meet certain standards or deadlines (e.g., tax compliance, environmental regulations)?
  3. Do you have any system decommissioning requirement like retaining legacy data or older systems for certain number of years?

9. End User and IT Support Impact

  1. What impact will the chosen approach have on end users, and how will training and adoption be managed?
  2. How dependent are your business processes on the current system’s user interfaces and workflows?
  3. What is the current state of your IT support structure? Does your IT team have the skills to support a Greenfield project, or are they more comfortable maintaining and testing the existing system in a Brownfield approach?

 

When you indulge in the dialog on these areas, you will bring out a general picture with data points to take the enterprise roadmap discussion ahead. You will capture data points and have constructive follow-on discussions to help create the business case. Situational and organizational knowledge plays a vital role. It can be difficult to decide immediately which SAP S/4HANA implementation strategy is right for you, however, the first step to define the business need and data to start building a business case will be achieved.

entry.title: Bill of Material (BOM)
entry.content:
Bill of Material (BOM) 

What is Bill of Material (BOM) and its role?

A bill of material is a complete, formally structured list of the components that make up a product or assembly. 

BOM can be single level and multi-level. For example, finished material would contain semi-finished materials as components which in turn would contain raw materials as components in next level.

Bill of Material having component number, description with quantity and unit measure.

There can be different alternative BOMs for a product depending upon the lot sizes, validity dates, and different production methods.

BOM is used in production planning for semi-finished and purchase planning for raw materials.

 

  • Use T code CS01 in SAP Easy Access

Enter Header Material and Plant, Select BOM Usage 1  Production

 

  • Enter the component that required to produce the product.

Quantity - Enter Material Quantity required

 

BOM Header

Tap on BOM Header to maintain the base quantity.

 

When we create more than 1 BOM of a product, it’s called Alternative BOM

When alternative BOM is more than 1 it’s called multiple BOM

BOM Status:

BOM Status should be 1 Active BOM which we ae going to use in transaction.

BOM Line Item

Select Item and select on item tab or double click on item.

 

Operation scrap refers to the expected material loss during a specific operation during production process.

When the material is a component, and is used as a part of an assembly and there is a loss when using that component. 

Recursive BOM can be understood as the material that uses the same header material for its make. You can also take an example of Curd we need curd to make curd so this is perfect example of Recursive BOM.

BOM Configuration:

1.Define default value [ Base Quantity Header and BOM Status]

To define default base quantity of header and default BOM status

 

 

 

  1. Determine the BOM Explosion mode

To define default Explosion Mode for BOM Header

Enable BOM Validity calculation

 

 2. Define BOM Usage

Define the BOM usage as per user requirement in which area use want to use

 

 

3. Define BOM Status

BOM status define for BOM availability for Transaction in various area like costing, plan order, Sales order etc.

 

 

  1. Define Allowed material type for BOM item

 To define Material type which we are going to use for BOM item in BOM usage 1 with respect to BOM header

BOM Like: FERT, HALB ETC.

BOM like: ROH, HALB RTC.

[ + Allowed, - Not Allowed]

 

 

BOM Reporting

T codes

CS11: Level by Level BOM Explosion

CS12: Multi Level BOM Explosion

CS13: Summarized BOM

CS14: BOM Comparison

CS15 : Where used material List

 

  • There might be the case wherein component material does not exist. For this, you need to create the material master for the component material before creating BOM.
  • Some users try to create the BOM again for the same Product, system gives warning message regarding “Alternative BOM2 is being created” and they tend to ignore the warning message and move forward and hence end up in creating similar alternative BOM 2 for same product. This unnecessary creates duplicate data.

Thankyou.

entry.title: Vendor Return Process.
entry.content:
What is return process - Return to supplier processing enables you to return damaged or unwanted products from your supplier.

Generally we are creating new return po to return the material.

Vendor return process with same PO this helps to return the stock with same price and also you can track the return in PO History.

Process :

  •  Create purchase order by using ME21N t code.
  • Create inbound delivery by using VL31N - By logistic department.

What is  inbound delivery - INBOUND DELIVERY in SAP pertains to all incoming goods, which may refer to either a vendor delivery or a return of a rejected delivery to a customer. It may cover the schedules of deliveries from your vendors/suppliers or even a rejected delivery to a customer. 

  • Create Transfer order - By using LT03 t code and confirm the transfer order by using LT12 t code 

The Transfer order or requirement contains information about a planned movement of stock in the warehouse. 

  • Post goods receipt - By using  MIGO t code 
  • Post Invoice receipt - By using MIRO t code procurement

Vendor Return Process with same PO 

  • Create Return Delivery against above posted GR document -- Check the flag via Delivery in MIGO Use Movement type 122/124
  • Display the Outbound delivery which was created - VL03N t code.

When you send any material out from the manufacturing plant it is called outbound delivery. While selling, you dispatch the material which is called outbound delivery.

  • Create transfer order and confirm the same - By using LT03 t code and confirm the transfer order by using LT12 t code 
  • Post Goods issue - By using VL02N 
  • Post credit Memo - By using MIRO t code

Upon receiving the returned goods, the vendor issues a credit memo. This document serves as a confirmation of the return and details the amount to be credited to the organization.

Differences between Vendor Return with same PO and Different return PO:

 

Vendor Return with same PO 

Vendor Return with new return PO

  • Return of the goods cab ne track in same PO history
  • Unable to link return po with original purchase order
  • Goods can be return with same price
  • Goods can be return with same or different price
  • Returns movement type are 122/124
  • Returns movement type are 122/124

 A well-documented return process supports compliance with regulatory requirements and internal controls.

entry.title: Re: SAP Datasphere - Do we need to keep using BW Bridge?
entry.content:
Your understanding is correct. After initial migration to BW Bridge, you can start rebuilding or recreating your data flows in the native SAP Datasphere environment by

Recreating data models and logic using native SAP Datasphere capabilities (like Data Flows, Graphical Views, or SQL-based models). Gradually transitioning away from BW Bridge-specific tools and processes.

You can Transition at your own pace, in a Phased approach. It could co-exist but SAP’s strategy is focused on making SAP Datasphere the primary environment for future innovations

entry.title: Re: How do I reset RSMDATASTATE after a load to a Datamar...
entry.content: When you say "load has been decommissioned" Can you elaborate? Do you still have transformation? if yes, you can create a new DTP or use existing DTP to perform an init without data transfer by setting the DTP processing mode as  'No Data Transfer; Delta Status in Source: Fetched'. Once you execute the load with this setting, the DataMart criteria will be fulfilled and you will be able to compress the request in source. Manually updating the DMALL field of RSMDATASTATE is not recommended.

entry.title: Re: Too many entries in smq1 with destination none
entry.content: The MCEX47 queue specifically handles data extraction for the Inventory Management module. Queues with entries marked as destination: none generally indicate that the system hasn't assigned a destination for data transfer, which could be a result of inactive or misconfigured delta data loads to BW. Chec if there is an inventory  delta data loads in BW system. Use transaction SMQ1 to check the contents of the queue MCEX47. Check if there are any unprocessed LUWs (Logical Units of Work) and determine whether the data has already been extracted or if it is still needed for BW.

Please choose below Diagnostic Agent installation files (DIAGNOSTICS AGENT 7.53) according to your Solution Manager version: 

 

 DIAGNOSTICS AGENT 7.53 ( DIAGNOSTICSAGENT.SAR on the Kernel Media )

  • Release 720 ( see table above)
  • Patch Level: latest ( >= the patch level mentioned in the table above)
  • From https://launchpad.support.sap.com/#/softwarecenter/template/products/_APP=00200682500000001943&_EVENT=DISPHIER&HEADER=Y&FUNCTIONBAR=N&EVENT=TREE&NE=NAVIGATE&ENR=73554900100200008550&V=MAINT

>= 7.2 SP12 

Download 720.14.21(released on 2024-OCT-07) when connecting against SAP Solution Manager >= 7.2 SP12

- SMDA720_SP14_21-80003641.SAR

 

>= 7.2 SP0 and <= 7.2 SP11

Download 720.11.22(released on 2024-JAN-23) when connecting against SAP Solution Manager >= 7.2 SP0 and <= 7.2 SP11:

  • SMDA720_SP11_22-80003641.SAR

 

The full installation strategy could be found in SAP Note2253383 - Diagnostics Agent - SWPM Archive-Based Installation

 

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Encoding Conversion Groovy Script
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I am now writing Groovy script for Encoding Conversion.

To be specific, a script for the conversion between Shift_JIS and UTF-8. 

I have completed the scripts that can do the one-way conversion from Shift_JIS to UTF-8, as well as the one-way conversion from UTF-8 to Shift_JIS.

However, when I want to write a two-way conversion script, it did not work well on Cloud Integration.

Could anyone kindly help me with this?

 

 

As your reference, here are the scripts that I wrote.

1)one-way conversion from Shift_JIS to UTF-8 (Worked Well)

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets

def Message processData(Message message) {
    // Get the body as a byte array to preserve the original encoding
    def bodyBytes = message.getBody(byte[])
    
    // Convert the Shift_JIS byte array to a UTF-8 string
    def utf8String = new String(bodyBytes, "Shift_JIS").getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8)
    
    // Set the converted UTF-8 byte array as the new body
    message.setBody(utf8String)
    
    // Set the output charset to UTF-8
    message.setProperty("CamelCharsetName", "UTF-8")
    
    return message
}

2)one-way conversion from UTF-8 to Shift_JIS (Worked Well)

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets

def Message processData(Message message) {
    // Get the body as a byte array (assuming it's in UTF-8)
    def bodyBytes = message.getBody(byte[])
    
    // Convert the UTF-8 byte array to a Shift_JIS string
    def shiftJisString = new String(bodyBytes, StandardCharsets.UTF_8).getBytes("Shift_JIS")
    
    // Set the converted Shift_JIS byte array as the new body
    message.setBody(shiftJisString)
    
    // Set the output charset to Shift_JIS
    message.setProperty("CamelCharsetName", "Shift_JIS")
    
    return message
}

 

3)two-way conversion between UTF-8 and Shift_JIS (Not Working Well)

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets

def Message processData(Message message) {
    // Define the source and target encodings here
    def sourceEncoding = "Shift_JIS"  // Change this to your source encoding
    def targetEncoding = "UTF-8"  // Change this to your target encoding
    
    // Get the body as a byte array
    def bodyBytes = message.getBody(byte[])
    
    def convertedBytes
    
    // Perform the conversion based on the specified encodings
    if (sourceEncoding == "Shift_JIS" && targetEncoding == "UTF-8") {
        convertedBytes = new String(bodyBytes, "Shift_JIS").getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8)
    } else if (sourceEncoding == "UTF-8" && targetEncoding == "Shift_JIS") {
        convertedBytes = new String(bodyBytes, StandardCharsets.UTF_8).getBytes("Shift_JIS")
    } else {
        // For other encoding combinations, use a more general approach
        convertedBytes = new String(bodyBytes, sourceEncoding).getBytes(targetEncoding)
    }
    
    // Set the converted byte array as the new body
    message.setBody(convertedBytes)
    
    // Set the output charset
    message.setProperty("CamelCharsetName", targetEncoding)
    
    return message
}

 

To add on, (3)two-way conversion between UTF-8 and Shift_JIS works well when it is SJIS to UTF-8.

However, it does not work when it is UTF-8 to SJIS.

 

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Scripting in Adapt UI for classic applications with Screen Personas
entry.content:
Collaborative effort of Smitha Koppar

This blog post will provide  information on how to do scripting in Adapt UI for classic applications with Screen Personas in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition.

Introduction

The SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition 2208 release brought several enhancements, including a new barcode scanning feature that improves operational efficiency and accuracy in inventory management and logistics. The barcode scanning functionality allows users to quickly and accurately capture data, reducing manual entry errors and speeding up processes like goods receipt, inventory counts, and order fulfilment. Integrating a barcode scanner into classic apps on tablets is a great way to streamline data entry! By adding a scan button to input fields, users can quickly capture information using the device’s camera, which can significantly reduce the time and effort required for manual entry.

The same can be seen in blog.

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/adapt-ui-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-2208-includes-new-barcode-scanning-feature/ba-p/13540869

Adapt UI and Processes

Lets see how this can be done in SAP S/4HANA Public cloud edition.

Use case with scripting

Let’s see a practical use case.

Customer says “I’ve added the scan button to the Order and Material field.  If there is no material field that appears, the Scan button is still visible… is it possible to set this so that it only appears if there is a material field that comes up?  “

The requirement can be achieved via scripting.

Scripting programmatically define the system behaviour for custom UI elements that were added through the Insert tab in the flavor manager.

For example, screens can be skipped and fields can be prefilled. SAP Screen Personas scripts are small pieces of JavaScript code that can be attached to individual UI elements with control events, or entire screens with screen events.

Below is small demo and logic for scripting for achieving customer requirement.

 

 

 

if (session.idExists("id of input field")) {

   session.findById("id of SCAN button").show();

} else {

   session.findById("id of SCAN button").hide();

}

 

 

 

References

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/adapt-ui-for-classic-applications-with-screen-personas-integral-part-of-the/ba-p/13564222

https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/screen-personas

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_SCREEN_PERSONAS/20993a0c28654a26beb2b63b722d74f2/8d43f46ac9e94b828b8e52e34d779310.html

__PRESENT

entry.title: Re: Some issues with the SuccessFactors Microsoft Teams i...
entry.content:
@StephenBurns 

  1. For the first point, a couple of comments
    1. "The app is unavailable due to user mapping issues. Please contact your administrator for help" - we do not disclose too much information to the end user so as to not reveal technical or configuration details to relieve them of this burden. We rely on admins who implemented the connection to have an impression that this could be a result of the user mapping they previously set up, since user mapping is one step in the configuration page. I do agree that for this message - it is useful to have it linked to an FAQ or enhance to reveal more info what the mapping issue refers to, we can investigate whether this option is possible.
    2. As for "unable to reach app", this is intended to be a generic message as the root cause cannot be differentiated and reflected in the message without troubleshooting, we display this generic message in the hopes of the user trying to access the app again, or asking for admins for help and eventually submit a Support ticket for engineers to take a closer look. We will explore the possibility of enhancing this message, or at least add some guidance for next step.
    3. Lastly, some of these error messages only occur under Application type permission. If you switch to Delegated type permission, there will be no user mapping issues since the login is triggered by the user themselves. You may see documentation about this type of set up here: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_SUCCESSFACTORS_PLATFORM/3adee415c1eb4b918bc0172afc76948c/7e014829b6684da3849fe2a4e2142f3a.html and a video demo here: https://sapvideo.cfapps.eu10-004.hana.ondemand.com/?entry_id=1_wtw6ptgu
  2. This seems to be config issue. If you don't have view pay statement configured in SuccessFactors, this will not shown up in the Teams App, we follow permissioning and configuration. As for the Help guide - we do need a space to display all supported actions, but would need to re-consider what else is the option here instead of exposing it to an end user. What would be your suggestion here - to remove the help link completely? In 2025, we are considering making the Help card text customizable. This is a good opportunity for you to remove the link there.
  3. Formatting issue is fixed. Please check again
  4. This is an issue that you could bring to the EC team, as we follow whatever template they provide.
  5. Is this related to #4? If so, feel free reach out to EC
  6. To my knowledge, you should be able to customize what is displayed in EC quick actions on the web. Have you tried reaching out to EC?
  7. Regarding people profile Teams chat link - this is on the Roadmap, please stay tuned.
  8. Sign out issue - this is currently expected behaviour that we don't allow a sign out under Application type permission. Is there a reason you need to sign out?  In addition if you chose to use Delegated type permission (like I mentioned in 1.3), we support a Connect/Disconnect command, which resembles and is purposed to mimic the act of sign in/sign out. 
  9. Home page and org chart - Which home page are you referring to? The Home tab in Teams? Currently, the labels are not customizable, but the content is. The content will by default show what you have configured. We display your HR Manager and your Cost Centre by default on the Home tab as we feel it is 'not easily found' information and would like to make it prominent for you when you need it. Regarding org chart - isn't this already a feature in Teams to view Organization structure? We do not feel the need to replicate this in the App if it is already available as part of the Teams core App.
  10. Customizable text - we have this planned to be able to customize the Help card in 2025. Please stay tuned.
  11. App message for when the App is downloaded by the user but App connection is not set up - we will investigate possible message enhancement there.
  12. Regarding lack of features - I would argue that we have plenty of feature as documented under the SuccessFactors App section of this guide: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_SUCCESSFACTORS_PLATFORM/3adee415c1eb4b918bc0172afc76948c/373e3d10260843b8bd0be1d556f4c762.html there are demo videos under each feature. Is there any other enhancement ideas or feature you feel would be useful in Teams, please feel free to submit Influence ideas under the Work Tech feature area here: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/3508

entry.title: \/SCWM/IM_PC transaction code, all line items are visible, including those that have not been staged
entry.content:
We have created a process order after which we perform partial staging using transaction COR2. However, I've noticed that in the /SCWM/IM_PC transaction code, all line items are visible, including those that have not been staged.

 

Ideally, we should only see the line item that has been staged. Because of this behavior every delivery document contains all the components which are already staged or needs to be staged. We get quantity as zero for the components which are not staged, however by this behavior our users get confused as all the materials show up every time and it is inconvenient as a human to check all the materials to find which has quantity more than zero.

 

Kindly help us on this process as we wish to only see the components which are staged while the delivery is created, this would help the users to efficiently bring in the required quantity for dispensing.

entry.title: Re: Accessing long text of SAP Master data Objects or fields in S4 Hana through a CDS View
entry.content:
@koyaajai You're right, the possible text objects are listed here. There's a workaround to extend the list to other text objects, proposed in the comments by @jeremiah_duroya (although not supported by SAP).

(as of S/4HANA 2023: /PF1/AC_CL, /PF1/AC_DL, /PLMB/RCP, APPROVAL, AUFK, CATS, CRM_ORDERH, CRM_ORDERI, C_SHES_TRI, DRAD, EKKO, EKPO, EQUI, ICM_BP, ICM_LOC, ICM_OBJ, ICM_REL, IFLOT, IMPTT, IMRG, INET, MATERIAL, MPCR, MPLA, MPOS, QMEL, RE, ROUTING, SCMG_CASE, WAPI, WBRK, WBRP, WCOCOH, WCOCOI).

@jeremiah_duroya Thank you, excellent workaround (how to extend this limited list of text objects, although as you say, the note 3048704 - Long text (SAPScript) search enablement for CDS-based search - SAP for Me says explicitly it's not supported: "3.Replication is supported only for SAP delivered models and not for Custom search models")

entry.title: Re: Hello Support I want to add Invoice With Branch Using...
entry.content:
Hi nitmanet168,

You can use the following sample code:

 

SAPbobsCOM.Documents lDocuments = (SAPbobsCOM.Documents)oCompany.GetBusinessObject(SAPbobsCOM.BoObjectTypes.oInvoices);
lDocuments.CardCode = "C20000";
lDocuments.Series = 3134;
lDocuments.DocType = SAPbobsCOM.BoDocumentTypes.dDocument_Items;
lDocuments.DocDate = new DateTime(2024, 07, 24);
lDocuments.DocDueDate = new DateTime(2024, 07, 24);
lDocuments.TaxDate = new DateTime(2024, 07, 24);
lDocuments.Comments = "Test via DI";
lDocuments.BPL_IDAssignedToInvoice = 18;
lDocuments.Lines.ItemCode = "A00001";
lDocuments.Lines.WarehouseCode = "01";
lDocuments.Lines.Quantity = 3.0;
lDocuments.Lines.TaxCode = "IGST18";
int AlDocuments = lDocuments.Add();

 

Where BPL_IDAssignedToInvoice is used to assign an active Branch.

Hope it helps!

Kind regards,

ANKIT CHAUHAN

SAP Business One Support

entry.title: Cannot join tables because Data in Columns : resb.sermat and resb.banfn fully blank
entry.content:
Hi everyone, In Tcode -IW39, I am trying to join the below tables to get data from the component. but I got an issue with column resb.sermat and resb.banfn is fully blank.
So what should I need to do to join these tables?
Best Regards

 

(from exsch_sap.ext_afih afih

left join exsch_sap.ext_aufk aufk on afih.mandt = aufk.mandt and afih.aufnr = aufk.aufnr

left join exsch_sap.ext_afko afko on aufk.mandt = afko.mandt and aufk.aufnr = afko.aufnr

left join exsch_sap.ext_iloa iloa on afih.mandt = iloa.mandt and afih.iloan = iloa.iloan)

left join exsch_sap.ext_resb resb on viaufks.mandt = resb.mandt and viaufks.swerk = resb.werks and viaufks.rsnum = resb.rsnum and viaufks.swerk = resb.werks

left join exsch_sap.ext_eban eban on viaufks.mandt = eban.mandt and eban.matnr = resb.matnr and eban.arsps = resb.rspos

left join exsch_sap.ext_eket eket on viaufks.mandt = eket.mandt and eban.bnfpo = eket.bnfpo and eban.banfn = eket.banfn)

entry.title: Re: Copy Measures across hierarchies member using Advance...
entry.content:
You should put scope into MEMBERSET, and just replace FLOW as needed, similar as below:

MEMBERSET [d/Account] = "C010101"
MEMBERSET [d/Product] = "TEST"
MEMBERSET [d/Plant] = "PR01"
MEMBERSET [d/Entity] = "ENT01"
MEMBERSET [d/OWNER] = "SPA"
MEMBERSET [d/Measure] = ("Value", "PERCENTAGE")
MEMBERSET [d/Flow] = "#"

DATA([d/Flow] = "01.01") = RESULTLOOKUP()

entry.title: Re: Differences between planned, schedule and actual date...
entry.content: Hello Ismael, You can refer to the help documentation: Maintaining Projects - Project Plan https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_BUSINESS_BYDESIGN/2754875d2d2a403f95e58a41a9c7d6de/2cdbd3eb722d1014aea1fabe56a9d37e.html?locale=en-US You can edit dates and durations for the project header or for project tasks. Based on your entered data, the system calculates the earliest and latest dates. All the dates are categorized into several groups: Planned Dates Scheduled Dates Actual Dates The displayed fields vary depending on whether the project header or project task is currently selected. The actual start date on the project task level is the date of the earliest recorded time confirmation via the time sheet; it is also editable for the project manager. The actual start date of the project is the earliest actual start date of all project tasks. When the project status is in planning, the scheduling is performed automatically by the system. When the project status is started or released, the scheduling has to be triggered manually by clicking Schedule. The actual finish date on project task level is editable for the project manager. The actual finish date of the project is the date when the project has been completed or closed (corresponds to the completion date of the project) or is the date when the project has been stopped. The duration of a phase or task can only be changed if there are no subordinate tasks. If the phase or task has subordinate tasks, its duration is automatically calculated by the system and cannot be changed. If the actual start/finish date on the project header level is populated, then Project Duration is automatically populated. There is no duration for milestones.

entry.content: Hello,
